narrative: As a Black male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.60—above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application stood out in some academic areas, but the admissions model ultimately predicted a 45% chance of failing the bar exam on your first try, tipping the decision toward rejection to prioritize students with lower predicted risks. This prediction draws from patterns in data on thousands of past students' academic records, family backgrounds, and exam outcomes, aiming to identify those most likely to succeed on the bar. The three biggest factors shaping this outcome for you were your undergraduate GPA, LSAT score, and first-year school decile ranking, in that order of influence. Your strong undergrad GPA was the most powerful positive force, pushing the prediction toward expecting you to pass by showing you have the academic foundation that often leads to bar success. On the flip side, your LSAT score of 28.50—well below the average of 36.70—was the top driver toward failure, as lower scores like yours have historically correlated with higher bar struggles in similar students. Your first-year decile of 4, under the typical 5.71 average, also helped counter the failure risk somewhat, indicating decent early performance relative to peers, though not elite. Compared to typical applicants, your full-time status aligns with the vast majority (over 92%), and your middle family income bracket is common, so they played smaller roles here. While your background as a Black male brings unique strengths and perspectives that law schools value, the model focused heavily on these quantifiable predictors, where your LSAT pulled hardest against you despite your GPA edge. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many overcome similar hurdles through targeted prep. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to or above average, as that single change could flip predictions for you dramatically. Strengthening early-grade deciles through additional coursework or highlighting upward trends might also help. Reach out to admissions advisors for feedback, and explore programs with holistic reviews that weigh your full story beyond these metrics—you've already shown GPA resilience that bodes well for bar prep with the right focus.
